shapes.name: TextBox 6
shapes.text: Newton's Laws of Motion

shapes.name: TextBox 10
shapes.text: Newton's Laws of Motion are a set of three fundamental laws that describe the relationship between an object's motion and the forces acting upon it.

shapes.name: TextBox 14
shapes.text: These laws explain the science behind a baseball player throwing a ball in the air.

texts.name: TextBox 6
texts.text: Newton's Laws of Motion

paragraphs.text: Newton's Laws of Motion

runs.text: Newton's Laws of Motion

texts.name: TextBox 10
texts.text: Newton's Laws of Motion are a set of three fundamental laws that describe the relationship between an object's motion and the forces acting upon it.

paragraphs.text: Newton's Laws of Motion are a set of three fundamental laws that describe the relationship between an object's motion and the forces acting upon it.

runs.text: Newton's Laws of Motion are a set of three fundamental laws that describe the relationship between an object's motion and the forces acting upon it.

texts.name: TextBox 14
texts.text: These laws explain the science behind a baseball player throwing a ball in the air.

paragraphs.text: These laws explain the science behind a baseball player throwing a ball in the air.

runs.text: These laws explain the science behind a baseball player throwing a ball in the air.

shapes.name: TextBox 9
shapes.text: The first law states that an object at rest will remain at rest, and an object in motion will continue in motion with the same speed and in the same direction, unless acted upon by an unbalanced force. This concept is also known as the law of inertia.

shapes.name: TextBox 13
shapes.text: A snowboarder gliding on ice will keep moving at a constant speed and direction until acted upon by an unbalanced force, like friction or an external force.

shapes.name: TextBox 15
shapes.text: First Law of Motion

texts.text: First Law of Motion

paragraphs.text: First Law of Motion

runs.text: First Law of Motion

shapes.name: TextBox 7
shapes.text: In the case of a curling stone sliding on ice, the unbalanced force acting upon it is friction, which slows it down until it stops.

shapes.name: TextBox 11
shapes.text: In basketball, the external force is the player's hand, which exerts a force on the ball to make it bounce back up after it hits the ground.

shapes.name: TextBox 12
shapes.text: The acceleration of an object is directly proportional to the net force acting on the object and inversely proportional to its mass. This means that the greater the force applied to an object, the greater its acceleration will be, and the heavier the object, the slower its acceleration will be.

shapes.name: TextBox 13
shapes.text: A baseball player can hit the ball farther and harder by swinging the bat with a greater force, and by using a lighter bat, which reduces the mass of the bat-ball system.

shapes.name: TextBox 14
shapes.text: Second Law of Motion

shapes.name: TextBox 11
shapes.text: When a hockey player hits a puck with their stick, the force of the impact is proportional to the mass and acceleration of the puck.

shapes.name: TextBox 12
shapes.text: When a basketball player shoots a ball, the force of the throw is proportional to the mass and acceleration of the ball.

shapes.name: TextBox 10
shapes.text: The third law states that for every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction. This means that if one object exerts a force on another object, the second object will exert an equal and opposite force back on the first object.

shapes.name: TextBox 14
shapes.text: When a skier pushes against the snow with their skis, the snow exerts an equal and opposite force back on the skier's skis, pushing the skier forward.

shapes.name: TextBox 15
shapes.text: Third Law of Motion

shapes.name: TextBox 7
shapes.text: In water flyboarding, the rider holds onto a hose that is connected to a watercraft, which provides the jet of water that lifts the board into the air.

shapes.name: TextBox 11
shapes.text: When an ice track skater pushes their foot backwards on the ice, the ice exerts an equal and opposite force forwards on the skater's foot.
